# Check if aide is already scheduled to run daily
if ! crontab -l | grep -q 'aide'; then
    # Schedule AIDE to run at 3 AM every day
    (crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo "0 3 * * * /usr/sbin/aide --check") | crontab -
fi

# Check if aide results are already being sent via email
if ! grep -q 'mail' /etc/aide.conf; then
    # Configure AIDE to send results to root's email
    cat <<EOF >>/etc/aide.conf

    # Create a script to parse AIDE log and send an email
    echo '#!/bin/bash' > /usr/local/bin/send_aide_email.sh
    echo 'LOG_FILE="/var/log/aide.log"' >> /usr/local/bin/send_aide_email.sh
    echo 'EMAIL_SUBJECT="AIDE Integrity Check Report"' >> /usr/local/bin/send_aide_email.sh
    echo 'EMAIL_TO="root"' >> /usr/local/bin/send_aide_email.sh

#!/bin/bash

# Set FIPS mode to enabled
setenforce 1

# Update system packages
dnf update -y

# Install required packages for FIPS compliance
dnf install -y nss-softokn-freebl libsecp256k1 openssl

# Configure OpenSSL to use FIPS compliant algorithms
sed -i '/^ssl_conf =/,/^}/ s|default_cipher_string = .*$|default_cipher_string = HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5:!RC4:!3DES:!eNULL:!EXP:!LOW:!MEDIUM:SSLv2:!SSLv3|' /etc/openssl/conf.d/ssl.conf

# Configure nss to use FIPS compliant algorithms
sed -i '/^fips_mode =/,/^}/ s|fips_mode = .*$|fips_mode = 1|' /etc/nss/nss.conf

# Reconfigure services that require FIPS compliance
systemctl restart httpd
systemctl restart https
systemctl restart ntpd
systemctl restart firewalld

# Ensure the system remains in FIPS mode after reboot
echo "enforcing=1" > /etc/sysconfig/enabled-services
```

#!/bin/bash

# Load system cryptographic policy
source /etc/crypto-policies/backends/system-policy

# Define FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers in order of strength
fips_ciphers="chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com,aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com,aes256-cbc,aes192-cbc,aes128-cbc,arcfour"

# Ensure SSH server configuration file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
    echo "SSH server configuration file not found. Creating default."
    cp /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.bak
    cat <<EOF > /etc/ssh/sshd_config
# Default SSH server configuration
HostKeyFiles /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
HostKeyAlgorithms ecdsa-sha2-nistp256,ecdsa-sha2-nistp384,ecdsa-sha2-nistp521
Ciphers $fips_ciphers
MACs hmac-sha2-512,hmac-sha2-256
KexAlgorithms curve25519-sha256@libssh.org,diffie-hellman-group-exchange-sha256
EOF
fi

# Apply the FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers to SSH server configuration
sed -i '/^Ciphers/s/.*/Ciphers $fips_ciphers/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

    sed -i '/^MACs/s:.*:hmac-sha2-256,hmac-sha2-512:' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
else
    echo "FIPS mode is not enabled on the system."
fi

# Reload SSH service to apply changes
systemctl reload sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Set up mandatory policy for smartcard removal to lock screen
POLICY_FILE="/etc/polkit-1/localauthority/50-local.d/99-smartcard-lock-screen.pkla"
if [ ! -f "$POLICY_FILE" ]; then
    cat <<EOF > "$POLICY_FILE"
[PolicyKit1]
Identity=unix-user:*
Action=org.freedesktop.screensaver.lock-screen
ResultCode=auth_adminok
EOF
fi

# Set up GNOME session to lock screen on smartcard removal
SESSION_FILE="/etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf"
if ! grep -q "smartcard-authentication-required" "$SESSION_FILE"; then
    sed -i '/^\[session\]$/a smartcard-authentication-required = true' "$SESSION_FILE"
fi

# Set up GNOME keyring to lock screen on smartcard removal
KEYRING_FILE="/etc/gnome-keyring/smartcard-lock-screen.gksrc"
if [ ! -f "$KEYRING_FILE" ]; then
    cat <<EOF > "$KEYRING_FILE"
[GNOME Keyring]
description=Lock screen when smartcard is removed
display-name=Smartcard Removal Lock Screen
enable-ssh-agent=true
enable-smartcard=true
lock-screen=true
name=smartcard-lock-screen
type=secrets
username=$USER
EOF
fi

# Restart necessary services to apply changes
systemctl restart gnome-session
systemctl restart polkit.service

    exit 1

#!/bin/bash

# Set the default timeout to 5 minutes (300 seconds)
SUDO_TIMEOUT=300

# Check if the sudoers file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/sudoers ]; then
    echo "sudoers file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Use visudo to edit the sudoers file with the specified timeout
visudo -c "%s Defaults timeout=$SUDO_TIMEOUT%s" /etc/sudoers

# Check if the command was successful
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "Failed to update sudoers file."
    exit 1
fi

echo "sudo credential caching timeout set to $SUDO_TIMEOUT seconds."

    # If it exists, check the current value of deny and update if necessary
    CURRENT_DENY=$(grep -oE "^auth\s+required\s+pam_tally2.so\s+onerr=succeed\s+deny=\K[0-9]+$" /etc/pam.d/system-auth)
    if [ "$CURRENT_DENY" -lt "$MAX_ATTEMPTS" ]; then
        sed -i "s/^auth\s+required\s+pam_tally2.so\s+onerr=succeed\s+deny=$CURRENT_DENY$/auth required pam_tally2.so onerr=succeed deny=$MAX_ATTEMPTS/" /etc/pam.d/system-auth
    fi
else

    echo "auth required pam_tally2.so onerr=succeed deny=$MAX_ATTEMPTS" >> /etc/pam.d/system-auth
fi

    # Update the lockout parameters in pam_unix.so configuration
    sed -i "/pam_unix.so/a account required pam_unix.so account_lock_time=$LOCK_TIME" /etc/pam.d/system-auth

fi

else
    echo "pam_tally2.so module is not used for accounting."
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define a function to check password quality
check_password_quality() {
    local oldpass="$1"
    local newpass="$2"

    # Calculate the number of different characters between the two passwords
    local diff_chars=$(echo -n "$oldpass" | grep -o . | sort | uniq | wc -l)

    # Check if the difference is at least 8 characters
    if [ $diff_chars -ge 8 ]; then
        return 0
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

# Create a new file to store the password history
if [ ! -f /etc/password_history ]; then
    touch /etc/password_history
fi

# Update the pam_pwquality module configuration
sed -i '/password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8/d' /etc/pam.d/common-password
echo "password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=8" >> /etc/pam.d/common-password

# Update the password history check function in pam_pwquality module
sed -i '/check_password_history/,$ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo "check_password_history = /usr/local/bin/check_password_quality" >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf

# Create the check_password_quality script
cat << 'EOF' > /usr/local/bin/check_password_quality
#!/bin/bash

OLDPASS=\$(grep -E "^$USER:" /etc/shadow | cut -d: -f1)
NEWPASS="\$1"

if ! check_password_quality "\$OLDPASS" "\$NEWPASS"; then
    echo "Error: New password must differ from the previous one by at least 8 characters."
    exit 1
fi

echo "\$NEWPASS" >> /etc/password_history
exit 0
EOF

# Make the script executable
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/check_password_quality

# Update the pam_pwquality module configuration to use the new script
sed -i '/check_password_history/,$ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo "check_password_history = /usr/local/bin/check_password_quality" >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf

# Loop through each user in the system

    # Check if the user is an interactive user
    if [ "$(id -u "$user")" != 0 ] && [ -n "$(grep -E "^$user:" /etc/passwd)" ]; then
        # Get the home directory of the user
        home_dir=$(eval echo ~"$user")

            if [[ ! " ${common_groups[*]} " =~ " $current_group " ]]; then
                # Get a common group that the user belongs to
                for group in "${common_groups[@]}"; do
                    if groups "$user" | grep -q "^$group "; then
                        # Set the correct group ownership
                        chgrp "$group" "$file"
                        break

for user in $(getent passwd | cut -d: -f1); do
    # Check if the user is an interactive user
    if [ "$(id -u "$user")" != 0 ] && [ -n "$(grep -E "^$user:" /etc/passwd)" ]; then
        # Get the home directory of the user
        home_dir=$(eval echo ~"$user")
        
        # Loop through each file and directory in the home directory
        find "$home_dir" -type f -o -type d | while read -r file; do
            # Check if the group ownership is not already correct
            current_group=$(stat -c "%G" "$file")
            if [[ ! " ${common_groups[*]} " =~ " $current_group " ]]; then
                # Get a common group that the user belongs to
                for group in "${common_groups[@]}"; do
                    if groups "$user" | grep -q "^$group "; then
                        # Set the correct group ownership
                        chgrp "$group" "$file"
                        break
                    fi
                done
            fi
        done
    fi
done

    local current_perms=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
    if [ "$current_perms" -gt "$REQUIRED_PERMS" ]; then

    fi

    find "$homedir" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name '.*' -exec bash -c 'check_and_set_perms "$0"' {} \;
done

root_homedir="/root"

#!/bin/bash

# Set the required permissions for dot-files to 0740
REQUIRED_PERMS=0740

# Function to check and set the correct permissions
check_and_set_perms() {
    local file="$1"
    local current_perms=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
    if [ "$current_perms" -gt "$REQUIRED_PERMS" ]; then
        chmod 0740 "$file"
    fi
}

# Loop through all users and check their dot-files
for user in $(getent passwd | cut -d: -f1); do
    # Skip the root user for now, as we'll handle it separately
    if [ "$user" = "root" ]; then
        continue
    fi

    # Get the home directory of the user
    local homedir=$(eval echo ~$user)

    # Check and set permissions for all dot-files in the home directory
    find "$homedir" -maxdepth 1 -type f -name '.*' -exec bash -c 'check_and_set_perms "$0"' {} \;
done

# Handle the root user separately to ensure it's included

#!/bin/bash

# Load existing kernel parameters
kernel_params=$(cat /boot/config-$(uname -r) | grep 'virtualization' | awk '{print $3}' | xargs)

# Disable virtual syscall capability
new_kernel_params="${kernel_params%,*} no_vsyscall"

# Update the current kernel configuration
sed -i "s/^virtualization.*/$new_kernel_params/" /boot/config-$(uname -r)

# Apply the same change to all installed kernels
for config_file in $(find /boot -name 'config-*'); do
    sed -i "s/^virtualization.*/$new_kernel_params/" "$config_file"
done

# Update GRUB configuration for future kernels
sed -i '/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT/s/.*$/\ \ no_vsyscall/' /etc/default/grub

# Re-generate GRUB configuration
grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg

#!/bin/bash

# Define the remote collector's certificate file path
REMOTE_CERT="/etc/ssl/certs/remote_collector.crt"

# Ensure the certificate exists
if [ ! -f "$REMOTE_CERT" ]; then
    echo "Remote collector certificate not found at $REMOTE_CERT"
    exit 1
fi

# Configure rsyslog to use SSL and certificate-based authentication
cat << 'EOF' | tee /etc/rsyslog.d/10-remote_collector.conf > /dev/null
module(load="imtcp")
input(type="tcp" port="5140" bind="127.0.0.1")

# Define the action for forwarding logs to the remote collector
action(type="omfwd"
       target="remote_collector:5140"
       streamDriver="ssl"
       streamDriverAuthMode="name"
       streamDriverCertFile="$REMOTE_CERT"
       StreamName="rsyslog_to_remote_collector"
       template(name="outfmt" type="string" string="%msg%\n")
)
EOF

# Restart rsyslog to apply the changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

BOOT_PARTITION="/boot"
MOUNT_OPTIONS="nosuid,nodev"

# Check if boot partition is mounted
if mountpoint -q "$BOOT_PARTITION"; then
    # Get current mount options for boot partition
    CURRENT_OPTIONS=$(mount | grep "$BOOT_PARTITION" | awk '{print $4}' | cut -d, -f1)
    
    # Check if nosuid and nodev are already set
    if [[ "$CURRENT_OPTIONS" != *"$MOUNT_OPTIONS"* ]]; then

        umount -l "$BOOT_PARTITION"
        mount -o remount,"$MOUNT_OPTIONS" "$BOOT_PARTITION"

    if [[ -e "$BOOT_PARTITION" ]]; then
        echo "Boot partition exists but is not mounted."

    fi
fi

if grep -qE "^$BOOT_PARTITION " /etc/fstab; then
    sed -i "s|^$BOOT_PARTITION .*$|${BOOT_PARTITION} ${MOUNT_OPTIONS}|g" /etc/fstab
else
    echo "${BOOT_PARTITION} ${MOUNT_OPTIONS} defaults 0 0" >> /etc/fstab
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define a function to apply the protection to each partition
apply_protection() {
    local mount_point="$1"
    # Check if the mount point is not root and is a local filesystem
    if [[ "$mount_point" != "/" && $(grep -q "^$mount_point " /etc/fstab; echo $?) == 0 ]]; then
        # Get the device associated with the mount point
        local device=$(df "$mount_point" | tail -1 | awk '{print $1}')
        # Check if the device is not a loopback or swap
        if [[ ! $(echo "$device" | grep -E 'loop|swap') ]]; then
            # Create a directory for device files under the mount point
            mkdir -p "$mount_point/dev"
            # Change ownership and permissions to prevent unauthorized access
            chown root:root "$mount_point/dev"
            chmod 755 "$mount_point/dev"
        fi
    fi
}

# Apply the protection to all non-root local partitions
while IFS= read -r line; do
    if [[ $(echo "$line" | grep '^/dev' | wc -l) == 0 ]]; then
        apply_protection "$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $2}')"
    fi
done < /etc/fstab

# Ensure the changes persist across reboots by creating a systemd drop-in file
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/systemd/system/mount.target.wants/protect-devices.service.d/10-protect-devices.conf
[Service]
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "for mount_point in \$(mount | grep '^ on ' | awk '{print \$3}'); do if [[ \"\$mount_point\" != \"/\" && \$(grep -q \"^$mount_point \" /etc/fstab; echo \$?) == 0 ]]; then mkdir -p \"\$mount_point/dev\"; chown root:root \"\$mount_point/dev\"; chmod 755 \"\$mount_point/dev\"; fi; done"
EOF

#!/bin/bash

# Define the known value to be written into freed memory pages
KNOWN_VALUE=0xDEADBEEF

# Function to update grub configuration for all installed kernels
update_grub() {
    local grub_config="/etc/grub2/grub.cfg"
    if grep -qE "linux.*poison_memory_space" "$grub_config"; then
        # Check if the correct value is already set
        current_value=$(grep -oP "linux.*poison_memory_space=\K[^ ]+" "$grub_config")
        if [ "$current_value" != "$KNOWN_VALUE" ]; then
            sed -i "s/poison_memory_space=[^ ]*/poison_memory_space=$KNOWN_VALUE/" "$grub_config"
        fi
    else
        # Add the poison_memory_space option with the known value
        local line_number=$(grep -n 'linux16' "$grub_config" | cut -d: -f1)
        if [ -z "$line_number" ]; then
            echo "Error: Unable to find 'linux16' in grub configuration."
            exit 1
        fi
        sed -i "${line_number}i linux16 poison_memory_space=$KNOWN_VALUE" "$grub_config"
    fi
}

# Update grub for all currently installed kernels
update_grub

# Function to update grub configuration when a new kernel is installed
on_new_kernel() {
    local new_kernel=$(echo "$1" | awk '{print $3}')

#!/bin/bash

# Define the known value to be written into freed memory pages
KNOWN_VALUE=0xDEADBEEF

# Function to update grub configuration for all installed kernels
update_grub() {
    local grub_config="/etc/grub2/grub.cfg"
    if grep -qE "linux.*poison_memory_space" "$grub_config"; then
        # Check if the correct value is already set
        current_value=$(grep -oP "linux.*poison_memory_space=\K[^ ]+" "$grub_config")
        if [ "$current_value" != "$KNOWN_VALUE" ]; then
            sed -i "s/poison_memory_space=[^ ]*/poison_memory_space=$KNOWN_VALUE/" "$grub_config"
        fi
    else
        # Add the poison_memory_space option with the known value
        local line_number=$(grep -n 'linux16' "$grub_config" | cut -d: -f1)
        if [ -z "$line_number" ]; then
            echo "Error: Unable to find 'linux16' in grub configuration."
            exit 1
        fi
        sed -i "${line_number}i linux16 poison_memory_space=$KNOWN_VALUE" "$grub_config"
    fi
}

# Update grub for all currently installed kernels
update_grub

# Function to update grub configuration when a new kernel is installed
on_new_kernel() {
    local new_kernel=$(echo "$1" | awk '{print $3}')
    if ! grep -qE "linux.*poison_memory_space" "/etc/grub2/$new_kernel/grub.cfg"; then
        # Add the poison_memory_space option with the known value to the new kernel's grub configuration
        local line_number=$(grep -n 'linux16' "/etc/grub2/$new_kernel/grub.cfg" | cut -d: -f1)
        if [ -z "$line_number" ]; then
            echo "Error: Unable to find 'linux16' in grub configuration for new kernel."
            exit 1
        fi
        sed -i "${line_number}i linux16 poison_memory_space=$KNOWN_VALUE" "/etc/grub2/$new_kernel/grub.cfg"
    fi
}

# Register the on_new_kernel function to be called when a new kernel is installed
echo "on_new_kernel /etc/kernel/postinst.d/custom-grub-poison-memory-space" > /etc/kernel/postinstall.d/99_custom-grub-poison-memory-space

exit 0

/com/example/bin/* flags=(attach_disconnected)

# Define the list of SSH-related files to check
ssh_files=(~/.ssh/authorized_keys ~/.ssh/config)

# Function to check file permissions and ownership
check_ssh_file() {
    local file=$1
    if [ -e "$file" ]; then
        # Check for world-writable permission
        if [ -w "$(dirname "$file")/$(basename "$file")" ] && ! [ -w "$file" ]; then
            echo "World-writable $file, changing permissions."
            chmod 600 "$file"
        fi

        # Check for correct ownership
        local owner=$(stat -c '%U' "$file")
        if [ "$owner" != "root" ]; then
            echo "Incorrect ownership of $file, changing to root."
            chown root:root "$file"
        fi
    else
        echo "SSH-related file $file does not exist, creating with correct permissions and ownership."
        mkdir -p "$(dirname "$file")"
        touch "$file"
        chmod 600 "$file"
        chown root:root "$file"
    fi
}

# Check each SSH-related file
for file in "${ssh_files[@]}"; do
    check_ssh_file "$file"
done

cron_entry="@reboot /path/to/this/script.sh"
if ! crontab -l | grep -q "$cron_entry"; then
    echo "$cron_entry" | crontab -
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Check if SSH server configuration file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
    echo "SSH server configuration file does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the original sshd_config file
cp /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.bak

# Read the current SSH server configuration
while IFS= read -r line; do
    # Check if the line contains 'LoginGraceTime'
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^LoginGraceTime ]]; then
        # Replace with new value if not already set
        echo "LoginGraceTime 10" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    elif [[ "$line" =~ ^PermitRootLogin ]]; then
        # Ensure root login is disabled for security
        echo "PermitRootLogin no" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    else
        # Keep the original line
        echo "$line" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    fi
done < /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Create a directory for USBGuard if it does not exist
mkdir -p /etc/usbguard

# Generate an empty policy file
cat > /etc/usbguard/policy.conf <<EOF
[General]
description = "USB device-authorization policy"

[RuleSet]
EOF

# Get the list of currently connected USB devices
connected_devices=$(lsusb | awk '{print $6}' | sort -u)

# Add rules for each connected device to the policy file
for device in $connected_devices; do
    echo "[RuleSet]" >> /etc/usbguard/policy.conf
    echo "description = \"Allow $device\"" >> /etc/usbguard/policy.conf
    echo "rules = [ Allow { DeviceVendorId = 0x$device, DeviceProductId = 0 } ]" >> /etc/usbguard/policy.conf
done

# Enable and start USBGuard to apply the policy
systemctl enable usbguard.service
systemctl start usbguard.service

#!/bin/bash

# Lock audit rules file to prevent changes during runtime
chmod 444 /etc/audit/audit.rules

# Create a lock file in /run to indicate that the audit configuration is immutable
touch /run/audit_immutable.lock

# Set the lock file permissions to only allow root access
chmod 400 /run/audit_immutable.lock

# Ensure the lock file persists across reboots by creating a systemd drop-in unit
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/audit_immutable.service.d/immutable.conf
[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "if [ ! -f /run/audit_immutable.lock ]; then touch /run/audit_immutable.lock; chmod 400 /run/audit_immutable.lock; fi"
EOF

# Reload the systemd manager configuration to apply the drop-in unit
sudo systemctl daemon-reload

#!/bin/bash

# Define the sudo configuration directory
SUDO_DIR="/etc/sudoers.d"

# Check if audit rule already exists
AUDIT_RULE="audit_rule_exists=$(grep -c " -w $SUDO_DIR -p wa" /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules)"
if [ "$AUDIT_RULE" -eq 0 ]; then
    # Add the audit rule to record write and attribute changes
    echo "-a always,exit -F path=$SUDO_DIR -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k sudo_modification" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules

#!/bin/bash

# Define the sudo configuration directory
SUDO_DIR="/etc/sudoers.d"

# Check if audit rule already exists
AUDIT_RULE="audit_rule_exists=$(grep -c " -w $SUDO_DIR -p wa" /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules)"
if [ "$AUDIT_RULE" -eq 0 ]; then
    # Add the audit rule to record write and attribute changes
    echo "-a always,exit -F path=$SUDO_DIR -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k sudo_modification" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
fi

# Update audit rules and reload the audit daemon
auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
systemctl restart auditd
```

# Define the audit rule for permission modification by users with login UID >= 1000
audit_rule="audit_rule -a always,exit -F arch=b32,b64 -S chmod -F auid>=1000 -F key=perm_mod"

# Check if auditctl is available and load the rule using auditctl-based rules

    echo "$audit_rule" | auditctl -M

config_file="/etc/audit/rules.d/perm_mod.rules"
echo "$audit_rule" > "$config_file"

# Ensure the audit daemon is restarted to apply the new rules
systemctl restart auditd

auditctl -w /sys/fs/cgroup/unified/uid_map -p warx -k extattr_remove

auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlink -F itemname=.* -F auid>=1000 -k extattr_remove

auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlink -F itemname=.* -F auid=0 -k extattr_remove
